text_blocks.text: As soon as the Greek War of
Independence broke out in 1821, several
Greek Cypriots left for Greece to join the
Greek forces. In response, the Ottoman
governor of Cyprus arrested and executed
486 prominent Greek Cypriots, including the
Archbishop of Cyprus, Kyprianos and four
other bishops. In 1828, modern Greece's first
president Ioannis Kapodistrias called for
union of Cyprus with Greece, and numerous
minor uprisings took place. Reaction to
Ottoman misrule led to uprisings by both
Greek and Turkish Cypriots, although none
were successful. After centuries of neglect by
the Turks, the unrelenting poverty of most
of the people, and the ever-present tax
collectors fuelled Greek nationalism, and by
the 20th century idea of enosis, or union,
with newly independent Greece was firmly
rooted among Greek Cypriots.
